Questions: What are Alexander Elder's Three Ms of successful trading?
Answers: Elder's Three Ms are Mind (psychology), Method (trading system), and Money (risk management). He argues that all three must be mastered simultaneously — a brilliant method with poor psychology will fail just as surely as a poor method with strong psychology. Of the three, Elder repeatedly places Mind first because emotional failures destroy accounts that technique alone cannot save.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: Why does Elder place psychology at the top of his trading hierarchy?
Answers: Elder places psychology first because even a trader with a reliable system and sound risk rules will self-destruct if their emotions override their plan. Fear causes premature exits; greed causes overtrading; denial prevents cutting losses. He writes that if a trader's mind is not in gear with the markets, there is no chance of making money regardless of the quality of the indicators used.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is Elder's most famous statement connecting the trader's mind to trading success?
Answers: Elder writes: 'The key to winning is inside your head.' This statement, which appears throughout his work, encapsulates his conviction that the mental dimension of trading — managing emotions, maintaining discipline, and confronting self-deception — is more decisive than any technical tool or system.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder describe the trader's greatest enemy?
Answers: Elder identifies the trader's greatest enemy as the person they see in the mirror every morning. External factors — bad brokers, market manipulation, bad luck — are minor compared to the internal enemies of greed, fear, impulsivity, and self-deception that most traders carry into every trade.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder mean when he calls trading 'a serious intellectual pursuit'?
Answers: Elder uses this phrase to contrast professional and amateur mindsets. Treating trading as a serious intellectual pursuit means approaching it with the same rigour, discipline, and long-term commitment as a demanding profession — not as a get-rich-quick game or a source of excitement. Emotional trading, he says, is lethal; intellectual discipline is the foundation of survival.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What three psychological goals does Elder set for a beginning trader?
Answers: Elder sets three sequential goals: first, long-term survival (staying in the game); second, a steady growth of capital; and third, making high profits. He teaches that most beginners reverse this order, focusing immediately on profits while ignoring survival — which is why most blow up their accounts in the first year.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – Trading for a Living (community reviews & quotes) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/104311.Trading_for_a_Living

Questions: How does Elder describe the psychological trap of overconfidence after early wins?
Answers: Elder observes that a dangerous pattern afflicts virtually every beginning trader: they win a few times early, conclude that trading is easy, and start taking excessively large risks. The beginner's early success is a trap — it creates the illusion of skill where only luck has operated — and the overconfident expansion of size that follows typically destroys the account.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the role of commitment in trading psychology?
Answers: Elder places 'deciding to be in the market for the long haul' as his first rule of psychological preparation. A trader who views each losing stretch as a potential reason to quit will never develop the resilience and pattern recognition that profitable trading requires. The commitment to remain a trader even 20 years from now creates the psychological foundation for learning from mistakes rather than abandoning the pursuit.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – Trading for a Living (community reviews & quotes) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/104311.Trading_for_a_Living

Questions: Why does Elder say 'emotional trading is lethal'?
Answers: Emotional trading is lethal because decisions made under the influence of fear, greed, or excitement violate the rational risk-reward calculations that make a system work. Each emotionally driven override of a rule introduces random variability that nullifies the edge of even the best system over time. Elder argues that the rule of trading as a 'serious intellectual pursuit' is the antidote.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the psychological significance of Elder's phrase 'plan a trade, trade a plan'?
Answers: This phrase encapsulates Elder's core psychological discipline. Making all trading decisions before the market opens — when the trader has full objectivity — means that execution during market hours requires no new psychological effort. The plan has already been made; the only remaining task is following it. This structure protects the trader from impulsive deviations triggered by real-time emotional reactions.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder describe the three pillars of successful trading?
Answers: Elder describes three pillars: analysing the balance of power between bulls and bears (Method), practising good money management (Money), and maintaining personal discipline to follow a trading plan without getting emotionally 'high' or depressed in the markets (Mind). All three must be present; remove any one and the structure collapses.
Label: Psychology
Source: Medium / Coinmonks – Trading for a Living by Alexander Elder | https://medium.com/coinmonks/trading-for-a-living-by-alexander-elder-fddd428eca5a

Questions: What does Elder say about traders who focus exclusively on technical systems?
Answers: Elder repeatedly observes that focusing exclusively on technical systems is the typical beginner mistake. A brilliant system provides no protection against the psychological failures — overtrading, cutting winners early, holding losers too long, abandoning rules after a loss — that destroy accounts. The beginner who ignores Mind while mastering Method has built a three-legged stool with two legs.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is Elder's view on the relationship between self-awareness and trading success?
Answers: Elder teaches that self-awareness — the ongoing practice of observing your own mental states, emotional triggers, and habitual patterns of reaction — is as important as market analysis. A trader who understands their own impulses can interrupt them before they become costly decisions. Without self-awareness, the same psychological mistakes repeat in an endless cycle regardless of how much technical knowledge is accumulated.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological importance of keeping a trading diary?
Answers: The trading diary is Elder's central tool for psychological development. Writing down the reasoning and emotional state at the time of every entry and exit forces honest self-reflection that is impossible when trades are remembered selectively. Over time, the diary reveals the trader's specific patterns of self-sabotage, enabling deliberate correction.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is Elder's view on the psychiatrist's core skill as applied to trading?
Answers: Elder identifies the common core of good psychiatry and good trading as the commitment to reality — seeing the world as it actually is rather than as one wishes it to be. Both disciplines demand that the practitioner confront uncomfortable facts without flinching, discarding fantasies and facing the evidence directly.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder's medical background shape his approach to trader psychology?
Answers: Elder trained as a psychiatrist and practiced clinically while developing his trading career. This background gave him direct experience with the patterns of denial, self-sabotage, addiction, and compulsion that characterise many patients — and which he recognised identically in losing traders. His psychological framework for trading is not theoretical; it is derived from clinical observation of human behavioural failure.
Label: Psychology
Source: Elder.com – About Dr. Elder | https://www.elder.com/about

Questions: What does Elder mean by 'a trader is the weakest link in any trading system'?
Answers: Even a well-designed, historically validated system can be undermined entirely by the human who operates it. The trader is the weakest link because they introduce discretion, emotion, and inconsistency into a process that works only when rules are followed precisely. Elder uses this insight to argue that reducing the trader's discretionary intervention — through pre-committed plans and mechanical rules — improves performance.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – Trading for a Living (community reviews & quotes) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/104311.Trading_for_a_Living

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological goal of a successful trader?
Answers: Elder writes that the paradoxical goal of a good trader is not to make money but to trade well. When the process is executed with discipline and analytical rigour, profits follow almost as a by-product. A trader obsessed with the dollar outcome distorts their decisions; a trader focused on process quality develops the consistency that produces profits.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder define 'discipline' in the context of trading psychology?
Answers: For Elder, discipline means following a pre-written trading plan without deviation — entering when the rules say enter, exiting when the rules say exit, and respecting risk limits even when every emotion argues otherwise. Discipline is not a personality trait you either have or don't have; it is a learnable practice built through structured routines, pre-commitments, and honest self-review.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say a trader must change to become a winner?
Answers: Elder states directly: 'Winners think, feel and act differently than losers. Strip away your illusions and change your old way of being, thinking and acting.' This transformation is not primarily technical — it is psychological. The loser who simply learns more indicators without changing their emotional patterns will remain a loser. Genuine psychological change is the prerequisite for lasting improvement.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – Trading for a Living (community reviews & quotes) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/104311.Trading_for_a_Living

Questions: What is the 'brain myth' in trading psychology according to Elder?
Answers: The brain myth is the fantasy that successful trading requires secret knowledge or special intelligence that ordinary people lack. Elder demolishes this belief, noting that many consistently profitable traders have modest formal education. The differentiating factor is not intelligence but the psychological capacity for discipline, self-awareness, and acceptance of reality.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the 'undercapitalisation myth' and why does Elder reject it?
Answers: The undercapitalisation myth is the belief that a trader fails only because their account is too small. Elder refutes this by pointing out that a losing trader will destroy a large account almost as quickly as a small one — because the problem is not capital but psychological: overtrading and poor emotional management. More capital without psychological change simply funds a larger version of the same destructive pattern.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the 'autopilot myth' in Elder's psychology framework?
Answers: The autopilot myth is the fantasy that trading can be fully automated — that a purchased mechanical system will generate profits without psychological engagement. Elder rejects this completely. Markets constantly evolve, and rigid automated systems eventually fail when conditions change. Only a psychologically engaged human can detect trouble and adapt. Believing in the autopilot fantasy is a way of evading the uncomfortable work of self-development.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: Why does Elder call fantasy the primary psychological enemy of the beginning trader?
Answers: Beginning traders arrive with fantasies about easy money, foolproof systems, and their own special insight. These fantasies are incompatible with the market's ruthless reality. Every fantasy creates a bias — toward a preferred outcome — that distorts the reading of evidence, delays exits from losing trades, and inflates position sizes beyond what risk discipline allows.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the 'cult of personality' trap Elder identifies for traders?
Answers: Traders under pressure seek a guru — an authority figure who will make decisions for them and relieve the psychological burden of independent judgment. Elder identifies this as a regressive fantasy: followers surrender their analytical responsibility to a charismatic personality. When the guru's predictions inevitably fail, the follower is left with neither a working method nor the self-developed judgment to replace it.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What are the three types of market gurus Elder identifies and why are they all psychologically dangerous?
Answers: Elder identifies three types: market cycle gurus (who achieve fame during a bull market that happens to confirm their theory), magic method gurus (who sell proprietary systems), and dead gurus (whose methods are promoted by others after their deaths). All are psychologically dangerous because they reinforce the fantasy that someone else holds the key to trading success — externalising responsibility away from the individual.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder describe the 'universal fantasies' that distort traders' thinking?
Answers: Drawing on his psychiatric training, Elder identifies several universal fantasies common to traders: that they were specially gifted at reading markets; that their losses were caused by external factors; that a bigger account would solve their problems; that automation would replace the need for discipline. Each fantasy is a defence mechanism that protects the ego from confronting the real cause of failure: emotional mismanagement.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder mean by 'trade with your eyes open'?
Answers: Trading with eyes open means basing every decision on current, objective evidence rather than on hope, assumption, or wishful thinking. It means accepting what the market is showing — including unfavourable signals — without filtering the data through the lens of what the trader wants to be true. Elder identifies this realistic orientation as a core psychological skill that separates professionals from amateurs.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is Elder's key insight from Dan Ariely's cheating experiment and how does it apply to trading?
Answers: Elder references a Duke University experiment where subjects who cheated on a test continued to predict inflated scores on subsequent tests where cheating was impossible — because they had convinced themselves they were genuinely intelligent. Elder uses this to illustrate how traders rationalise past luck as skill, leading to dangerous overconfidence. The equity curve and trading journal, unlike self-reported memory, cannot be deceived.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological trap of mistaking luck for skill?
Answers: Early winning trades, often the result of random luck in a favourable market environment, create a powerful psychological illusion of skill. The trader who believes their first few wins reflect analytical ability rather than market conditions will take progressively larger risks — until the market delivers the losses that expose the illusion. Elder teaches traders to evaluate themselves through long-run statistical evidence, not recent emotional memory.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder use the 'overweight dieter' analogy to describe self-deception in trading?
Answers: Elder describes a woman he knew who claimed to be dieting and avoided eating in front of others but consumed large amounts of food in private. He uses this analogy to describe traders who say they will be disciplined but violate rules the moment no one is watching. The self-deception is identical: outward compliance conceals private behaviour that undermines the stated goal.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about wishful thinking and its effect on market analysis?
Answers: Wishful thinking corrupts market analysis by making traders see what they want to see rather than what is actually there. A bullish trader 'finds' bullish patterns; a bearish one 'finds' bearish ones. Elder teaches that the antidote is rule-based, objective analysis — writing down specific criteria before looking at the chart, then checking whether those criteria are met, rather than letting emotional desire shape the interpretation.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say is the psychological difference between an investor and a gambler?
Answers: An investor takes calculated risks based on analytical edge and sound money management, with clearly defined criteria for entry and exit. A gambler takes risks primarily for the emotional experience — the excitement of action and the hope of winning — without regard to the quality of the odds. Elder warns that the market provides the perfect camouflage for gambling behaviour, making it look like investment analysis.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the three types of gamblers and which one describes most failing traders?
Answers: Elder cites psychiatrist Dr. Ralph Greenson's three types: the normal person who gambles occasionally for entertainment, the professional gambler who views it as a business, and the neurotic gambler driven by unconscious compulsive needs. Most failing traders fall into the third category — they experience a compulsive urge to trade, find it difficult to stop, and are unconsciously seeking the emotional high of winning rather than rational long-term profit.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about gambling as 'an addiction without a drug'?
Answers: Elder quotes psychiatrist Dr. Sheila Blume's description of gambling as 'an addiction without a drug' to emphasise that compulsive trading can produce the same addictive cycle as substance abuse: the craving for action, the high of a win, the denial of the problem, and the inability to stop despite mounting losses. This framing helps traders recognise patterns in themselves that they might otherwise dismiss as normal market behaviour.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder explain why trading is 'the most dangerous human endeavour short of war' psychologically?
Answers: Most professions include colleagues, managers, or clients who provide informal safety nets — warning a person when their behaviour is self-destructive. Trading has no such external protection. Every other market participant is actively trying to take your money. This makes the market uniquely merciless: psychological weaknesses that are managed or overlooked in other contexts are punished immediately and financially.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the 'Demolition Derby' section of Elder's book about psychologically?
Answers: The Demolition Derby section explains that trading is like a highway where everyone is actively trying to crash into each other. Unlike driving, where road users try to avoid collisions because crashes harm both parties, in trading one party's gain is another's loss. This adversarial reality means a trader's psychological weaknesses — impatience, overconfidence, fear — are immediately exploited by more experienced participants.
Label: Psychology
Source: Bookey – Trading for a Living Chapter Summary | https://www.bookey.app/book/trading-for-a-living

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological pattern of blaming external factors for losses?
Answers: Elder identifies external blame — attributing losses to bad brokers, market manipulation, economic news, or bad luck — as the primary defence mechanism of losing traders. It protects the ego from the uncomfortable recognition that the cause of persistent losses lies within the trader's own behaviour. Until this defence is overcome and personal responsibility is accepted, genuine improvement is impossible.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What psychological story does Elder tell about the trader who kept losing despite correct analysis?
Answers: Elder describes a prominent trader whose equity was being destroyed by a dollar rally while he was heavily short. This trader had grown up fighting an abusive, domineering father and had made his reputation betting against established trends. Elder's analysis was that the trader could not admit the market — which unconsciously represented his father — was bigger and stronger than he was. He kept adding to his short position because psychologically he could not accept defeat.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder connect childhood psychology to adult trading behaviour?
Answers: Elder explicitly warns that mental baggage from childhood — unresolved conflicts, patterns of self-defeat, authority issues, compulsive behaviour — plays out in the markets. The trader who grew up learning that taking a loss means personal failure will hold losers indefinitely; the one who learned impulsivity will overtrade; the one with authority issues will defy market signals that symbolically represent rules. The trading journal is one tool for identifying these deep patterns.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the parallel Elder draws between alcoholism and losing trading?
Answers: Elder draws an extended parallel between alcoholics and losing traders. Both deny the problem despite mounting evidence; both switch tactics (from whiskey to beer, from one system to another) rather than addressing the root behaviour; both experience a compulsive urge they cannot resist; and both need to 'hit rock bottom' before they are ready to confront the real cause of their failure. The insight that allowed Elder to apply AA's recovery framework directly to trading.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the 'Losers Anonymous' concept Elder proposes?
Answers: 'Losers Anonymous' is Elder's metaphorical self-help framework for traders who recognise they have been trading compulsively and self-destructively. Just as AA's first step requires admitting powerlessness over alcohol, Elder's first step for the losing trader is admitting: 'Good morning, my name is [X] and I am a loser. I have it in me to do serious financial damage to my account.' This honest acknowledgement is the prerequisite for any genuine change.
Label: Psychology
Source: Collin Seow – Book Review: Trading for a Living (Part 1) | https://www.collinseow.com/book-review-trading-for-living-1/

Questions: What is the key lesson Elder took from attending an Alcoholics Anonymous meeting?
Answers: Elder attended an open AA meeting and was struck by the realisation that if he substituted the word 'loss' for 'alcohol' in every sentence, the members seemed to be describing his own trading struggles exactly. He left with the insight that his trading losses needed to be managed the way AA manages alcoholism: through strict rules, honest self-assessment, peer accountability, and incremental daily discipline.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the 'First Step' of AA and how does Elder apply it to trading?
Answers: AA's First Step requires admitting powerlessness over alcohol and acknowledging that one's life has become unmanageable. Elder applies this directly: the losing trader must admit powerlessness over the impulse to trade compulsively, and acknowledge that their trading account has become unmanageable. Only after this honest admission — which most traders resist vigorously — can the process of genuine change begin.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder mean by 'rock bottom' for a trader?
Answers: A trader hits rock bottom when accumulated losses, humiliation, and pain finally penetrate their denial — when they can no longer pretend the problem is external and must confront that the problem lies within their own behaviour. Some traders hit rock bottom after a few weeks; others spend years adding money to their accounts to postpone the reckoning. Elder regards this painful moment as a potential turning point: the threshold of genuine transformation.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the psychological significance of 'one day at a time' for traders according to Elder?
Answers: The AA slogan 'one day at a time' translates directly to trading discipline. Planning to follow rules perfectly for a trading career can seem overwhelming; planning to follow rules perfectly today is achievable. Elder advocates this incremental framing because it reduces the psychological burden of long-term commitment to manageable daily units — and because each successfully disciplined day builds the habit that makes the next day easier.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about denial in the context of losing traders?
Answers: Elder identifies denial as the central psychological mechanism that keeps losing traders in their destructive pattern. Just as an alcoholic denies that drinking controls their life even as it destroys everything around them, a losing trader denies that their emotional behaviour — not external factors — is responsible for their losses. The wall of denial cannot be penetrated by logic or other people's advice; it yields only to the pain of sufficient loss.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: Why does Elder say switching from one trading system to another is like an alcoholic switching from whiskey to beer?
Answers: Switching systems addresses the symptom (the specific method used) while leaving the root cause intact (the emotional behaviour that misapplies any method). An alcoholic who switches drinks remains an alcoholic; a trader who switches systems while maintaining the same emotional patterns remains a losing trader. The system is never the primary problem; the psychology is.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the 'urge to trade' as analogous to addiction?
Answers: Elder identifies the compulsive urge to be in the market — to place a trade simply because time has passed or because action feels necessary — as equivalent to an addict's craving. Just as an alcoholic cannot stop at one drink, the compulsive trader cannot stop at a planned number of trades. The 6% Rule's forced monthly pause is Elder's practical mechanism for interrupting this addictive cycle.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological similarities between losing traders and alcoholics?
Answers: Elder lists the following shared patterns: both deny the problem while their lives deteriorate; both try to manage the unmanageable; both hide evidence of their behaviour (alcoholics hide bottles; traders throw away brokerage statements); both try to recover the high by intensifying the behaviour (drinking more, trading larger); and both need external rules to impose the limits they cannot impose on themselves.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder recommend a trader use the AA principles in practice?
Answers: Elder recommends that traders on a losing streak attend an open AA meeting — not because they are necessarily alcoholics, but because hearing the identical psychological patterns described in a different context provides a jolt of recognition that can cut through denial. The specific recommendations are: admit powerlessness over the compulsive urge; make a daily decision to trade according to rules; keep a diary as a form of personal inventory; and maintain accountability to a trading partner or mentor.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the psychological function of trading rules according to Elder?
Answers: Trading rules serve the same psychological function as AA's 12 steps: they provide a structure that removes certain decisions from the realm of emotional discretion. When the rule says 'exit when the stop is hit,' the trader does not need to make that decision in the heat of the moment — it was already made. Rules convert potentially emotional decisions into automatic responses, reducing the psychological load at the most vulnerable moments.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological profile of a chronic loser?
Answers: The chronic loser: keeps no records and avoids reviewing losses; switches from system to system looking for an external solution; blames losses on external factors; doubles down on losing positions to recover the position; experiences a gambler's high from trading activity itself; and hits rock bottom repeatedly without changing the underlying behaviour. Elder's framework is explicitly designed to interrupt this pattern at the level of daily habits rather than grand intentions.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does 'hitting rock bottom' psychologically enable a trader to do?
Answers: Hitting rock bottom — when the pain of continued self-destructive trading exceeds the psychological cost of confronting it — enables the trader to take Elder's equivalent of AA's first step: genuine, non-defensive acknowledgement of the problem. Without this painful recognition, the mind's defences remain intact, rationalising each new loss and preventing the behavioural change that recovery requires.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder describe the recovery process for a psychologically damaged trader?
Answers: Elder's recovery process mirrors the AA model: (1) honest acknowledgment of the problem; (2) acceptance of personal responsibility rather than external blame; (3) adoption of a structured daily routine based on rules; (4) use of the trading journal as a daily inventory of behaviour and emotions; (5) gradual rebuilding of the account at reduced size to practice disciplined execution without the pressure of large financial stakes.
Label: Psychology
Source: Bookey – Trading for a Living Chapter Summary | https://www.bookey.app/book/trading-for-a-living

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological function of the daily morning ritual for traders?
Answers: Elder endorses the 'Losers Anonymous' daily affirmation — acknowledging one's capacity for self-destructive trading at the start of each session — as a psychological priming exercise. This momentary daily confrontation with one's vulnerabilities interrupts the complacency that leads to rule violations. It reactivates the disciplines learned and reminds the trader that the battle with their own impulses is ongoing.
Label: Psychology
Source: Collin Seow – Book Review: Trading for a Living (Part 1) | https://www.collinseow.com/book-review-trading-for-living-1/

Questions: What does Elder say about the risk of the first few winning trades for a recovering loser?
Answers: Elder warns that a string of winning trades after a period of disciplined recovery is itself a psychological danger. Success breeds the same overconfidence that originally destroyed the account. The recovering trader must treat a winning streak with the same discipline as a losing one — maintaining position size, following rules, and resisting the temptation to conclude that the psychological work is done.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder apply AA's concept of 'character defects' to trading failures?
Answers: AA's character defects — the patterns of thinking and feeling that make healthy relationships and self-management difficult — translate directly to trading defects: arrogance, denial, impulsivity, dishonesty with oneself, the inability to tolerate uncertainty or loss. Elder teaches that identifying and working on these defects, through honest journaling and systematic rule-following, is the essential psychological work of becoming a professional trader.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the emotional states that should trigger an immediate trading halt?
Answers: Elder states clearly: 'If you become aware of fear, greed, or a gambler's high, close your trades.' These emotional states impair judgment irreversibly for the duration of the session. The rational response is not to try to override the emotion but to remove the financial stake — exit all positions and disengage from the screen until emotional equilibrium is restored.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the psychological insight Elder gained from his personal trading crisis that changed his approach?
Answers: Elder describes running his trading account into the ground multiple times before the breakthrough insight: 'the key to winning was inside my head and not inside a computer.' Up to that point he had searched for better systems, better indicators, and better data. The recognition that his psychology — not his method — was the limiting factor is the central pivot of his entire trading philosophy.
Label: Psychology
Source: Elder.com – About Dr. Elder | https://www.elder.com/about

Questions: What is Elder's definition of the financial market from a psychological perspective?
Answers: Elder defines the market not as a mathematical system or a rational pricing mechanism but as a crowd of people — each acting on their individual fear, greed, hope, and panic. Every price tick represents the momentary consensus of this crowd. Technical analysis, in Elder's framework, is applied social psychology: reading the collective emotional state of all market participants at any given moment.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder mean when he says 'technical analysis is a study of mass psychology'?
Answers: Elder teaches that charts do not record abstract mathematical patterns — they record the decisions of real people made under conditions of uncertainty and emotional pressure. Moving averages reflect the crowd's average opinion of value; oscillators reflect its emotional extremes of greed and fear; divergences reveal moments when the crowd is becoming internally inconsistent. Understanding this makes the chart reader a student of group behaviour rather than a mathematical forecaster.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder explain why prices move?
Answers: Elder states that prices move up or down because of changes in the intensity of greed and fear among buyers and sellers — not because there are more buyers than sellers in terms of quantity (the number of stocks bought and sold is always equal). A price advances when buyers are more intensely motivated than sellers, even if equal in number. This psychological intensity, not arithmetic supply and demand, is the force that moves markets.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – Trading for a Living (community reviews & quotes) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/104311.Trading_for_a_Living

Questions: What does Elder say about the 'crowd' and its intelligence?
Answers: Elder writes that 'the crowd may be stupid, but it is stronger than you.' This apparent paradox — a crowd can be wrong about direction while being unstoppably powerful in the short run — is essential to trading psychology. A trader who correctly identifies the crowd as wrong but enters too early will be run over by the crowd's momentum before the eventual reversal vindicates their analysis.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – Trading for a Living (community reviews & quotes) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/104311.Trading_for_a_Living

Questions: How does Elder describe the psychological mechanism by which support and resistance are created?
Answers: Elder explains that support and resistance exist because traders have memories and feel pain and regret. Traders who bought at a previous support level remember the price and are relieved to get out 'at even' when price returns there, creating selling pressure. Traders who missed a previous rally feel regret and rush to buy when price returns to that level, creating buying pressure. These psychological memories are what make price levels 'sticky.'
Label: Psychology
Source: AllBookStores – The New Trading for a Living (reviews) | https://www.allbookstores.com/The-New-Trading-Living-Psychology/9781118443927

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological state of 'crowd members'?
Answers: Crowd members surrender their individual judgment to the group's momentum. When prices are rising strongly, individuals override their private doubts and buy because 'everyone else' is buying. This collective behaviour amplifies both advances and declines far beyond what fundamentals might justify. Elder teaches traders to remain psychologically separate from the crowd — using indicators to measure crowd sentiment as an external observable rather than surrendering to it.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder explain 'bulls, bears, hogs, and sheep' as psychological archetypes?
Answers: Elder uses these Wall Street archetypes to describe psychological types: bulls profit from rising markets; bears from falling markets; hogs get slaughtered by greed (holding too long or sizing too large); and sheep are led by the crowd, following tips and gurus without independent analysis. The hog and sheep archetypes represent the dominant psychological failure modes of retail traders.
Label: Psychology
Source: Barnes & Noble – Trading for a Living | https://www.barnesandnoble.com/w/trading-for-a-living-alexander-elder/1111460382

Questions: What is the psychological significance of 'each price representing the consensus of the crowd'?
Answers: Every price at which a transaction occurs is, by definition, the point at which one party's fear of holding is exactly balanced by another's desire to hold. This consensus is psychological — neither purely rational nor random. Understanding that price represents a crowd's momentary agreement allows the technically skilled trader to read the crowd's changing emotional state through price patterns and indicator behaviour.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – Trading for a Living (community reviews & quotes) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/104311.Trading_for_a_Living

Questions: How does Elder describe the psychology of a market trend?
Answers: A market trend reflects a sustained consensus among the crowd — buyers consistently more motivated than sellers over a period. Trends are born in uncertainty (when the new consensus is still forming), develop as the crowd builds confidence, and die in euphoria (when everyone is convinced the trend will continue indefinitely). The final stage of crowd consensus is also the most dangerous entry point for late buyers.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological creation of a market bubble?
Answers: A bubble forms when crowd psychology enters a feedback loop: rising prices attract new buyers whose purchases push prices further, validating the crowd's belief that prices will always rise. Elder teaches that the technical indicators — particularly divergences between price and momentum oscillators — are the objective evidence that crowd psychology has reached the euphoric extreme that precedes reversal.
Label: Psychology
Source: ACY Securities – Mass Psychology in Trading: Why Price Moves the Way It Does | https://acy.com/en/market-news/education/mass-psychology-why-price-moves-the-way-it-does-j-o-20251226-105138/

Questions: What is Elder's 'managing versus forecasting' distinction in terms of crowd psychology?
Answers: Elder advocates managing responses to current crowd behaviour rather than forecasting what the crowd will do. A trader who forecasts the future commits psychologically to a predicted outcome — and will resist exiting when the market contradicts their prediction. A manager who reads current crowd behaviour and responds to what is actually happening has no forecast to defend and can exit immediately when the evidence changes.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder explain why prices often overshoot rational fair value?
Answers: Prices overshoot because crowds are not rational in the short term. When fear or greed becomes dominant, the crowd's momentum carries prices beyond any fundamental justification. Elder teaches that this overshoot is predictable from the indicators — oscillators reaching extreme levels, Force Index diverging from price — providing the psychologically aware trader with identifiable exit and entry opportunities at the crowd's emotional extremes.
Label: Psychology
Source: ACY Securities – Mass Psychology in Trading: Why Price Moves the Way It Does | https://acy.com/en/market-news/education/mass-psychology-why-price-moves-the-way-it-does-j-o-20251226-105138/

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of breakouts and why they often fail?
Answers: Elder teaches that breakouts attract late crowd participation — traders who have been waiting for confirmation rush in after the break. This influx of crowd buying at an already-extended level is precisely what professionals fade: they sell into the crowd's enthusiasm at overextended prices. Understanding that a breakout represents peak crowd consensus, not a beginning, changes the psychological interpretation of this common pattern.
Label: Psychology
Source: ACY Securities – Mass Psychology in Trading: Why Price Moves the Way It Does | https://acy.com/en/market-news/education/mass-psychology-why-price-moves-the-way-it-does-j-o-20251226-105138/

Questions: How does Elder describe 'winners feeling rewarded and losers feeling punished' by price movement?
Answers: Elder writes that winners feel rewarded when price moves in their favour and losers feel punished when it moves against them. This emotional experience — essentially operant conditioning at the trading terminal — produces irrational behaviour: winners become overconfident and take excessive risks; losers become anxious and exit prematurely or double down. Professionals insulate their decisions from this reward-punishment cycle by focusing on process rather than P&L during the trading session.
Label: Psychology
Source: Medium / Coinmonks – Trading for a Living by Alexander Elder | https://medium.com/coinmonks/trading-for-a-living-by-alexander-elder-fddd428eca5a

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological state required to identify market turns accurately?
Answers: Accurately identifying crowd turning points requires a psychological state that Elder describes as 'cool, calm, and collected.' The trader must be emotionally separated from the crowd's current euphoria or panic — which requires that they have done their analytical work in advance, have clear rules for what constitutes a turn, and have no open position that would bias their reading of the evidence.
Label: Psychology
Source: Barnes & Noble – Trading for a Living | https://www.barnesandnoble.com/w/trading-for-a-living-alexander-elder/1111460382

Questions: What is Elder's view on the psychological relationship between the trader and the market crowd?
Answers: Elder teaches that the market crowd is a force the trader must observe and respond to — not join. Profitable trading requires standing apart from the crowd's emotional extremes, entering when the crowd is panicking (in a bullish tide) and exiting when the crowd is euphoric. This requires a psychological stance of deliberate non-conformity: the willingness to act against current sentiment based on analytical evidence.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological trap of following other traders' tips?
Answers: Following tips and advice from others — gurus, newsletters, chat rooms — is psychologically seductive because it externalises the responsibility for trading decisions. If the tip fails, the advisor is to blame. Elder teaches that this externalisation is both psychologically damaging (it prevents the development of independent judgment) and practically ineffective (by the time a tip reaches a retail trader, the professional who generated it has usually already acted on it).
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder explain why 'prices trend because the dominant mass grows more optimistic or pessimistic'?
Answers: Elder uses the EMA as a concrete expression of this principle. When a rising EMA confirms an uptrend, it is measuring the crowd's growing optimism — buyers are consistently willing to pay more over time. When an oscillator shows the crowd briefly more pessimistic (the daily pullback), it signals a temporary emotional dip within the dominant optimism — the ideal psychological moment to align with the crowd's underlying bullish orientation.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder say professionals differ from amateurs in their relationship to crowd emotions?
Answers: Amateurs are swept up by crowd emotions — buying when excitement peaks and selling when fear peaks — producing buy-high-sell-low behaviour. Professionals use crowd emotional extremes as contrary indicators: overbought sentiment is a signal to prepare to sell; oversold panic is a signal to prepare to buy. The professional's psychological separation from crowd emotion is what creates the contrarian edge.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder mean by 'price is what the greater fool is ready to pay'?
Answers: This quote, which Elder uses to describe speculative bubbles, captures the psychological dynamic of crowd-driven price inflation: price has no fundamental anchor when crowd psychology is driving it — it reflects only what the next buyer (the 'greater fool') is willing to pay. Elder uses this concept to reinforce the importance of selling when crowd enthusiasm is extreme rather than holding on the assumption that yet another buyer will arrive at an even higher price.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – Trading for a Living (community reviews & quotes) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/104311.Trading_for_a_Living

Questions: What does Elder say about technical analysis as 'applied social psychology, like poll-taking'?
Answers: Elder draws this parallel to explain that chart analysis does not predict the future based on mathematical laws — it surveys the current emotional state of the market crowd, just as a political poll surveys voter sentiment. Support, resistance, breakouts, and chart patterns are psychological phenomena — the visible traces of crowd behaviour — not physical constants. Understanding them as psychological rather than mathematical makes the trader a social analyst rather than a forecaster.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological damage caused by overconfidence?
Answers: Overconfidence following a winning streak is one of Elder's most frequently cited psychological dangers. After consecutive wins, traders believe they have mastered the market and start deviating from their rules — increasing size beyond the 2% limit, skipping the second-screen confirmation, holding winners too long for more. This psychological hubris sets up the devastating loss that wipes out weeks of gains.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of holding a losing position?
Answers: Holding a losing position is almost always rooted in ego — the refusal to admit the trade is wrong. The pre-set stop-loss is the technical solution to what is fundamentally a psychological problem. Elder teaches that a loss accepted when it is small, in accordance with a pre-made plan, is a rational business decision. A loss that is allowed to grow because of hope or denial is an emotional failure that can become catastrophic.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of exiting winners too early?
Answers: Exiting winners prematurely is driven by fear — specifically the fear that the profit will be taken away. This fear leads traders to lock in small gains before the trade has reached its technical target, producing a pattern where losses are held too long (hoping for recovery) and winners are cut short (afraid of reversal). Elder addresses this asymmetry directly through pre-defined targets and the rule of not moving stops against the position.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is Elder's concept of the 'emotional roller coaster' and how does he recommend managing it?
Answers: Elder describes most traders as riding an emotional roller coaster: elated during winning periods, despairing during losses, making trading decisions based on their current emotional state rather than systematic analysis. The management requires detaching P&L awareness from real-time trading. He recommends tracking grades of trade quality rather than dollar outcomes, so that the focus is on process excellence rather than moment-to-moment profit fluctuation.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – The New Trading for a Living (reviews) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/22505444-the-new-trading-for-a-living

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of revenge trading?
Answers: Revenge trading — the compulsive urgency to place a new trade immediately after a loss to 'get the money back' — is one of the most destructive psychological patterns Elder identifies. It drives the trader into positions with poor timing, excessive size, and inadequate analysis — all under the influence of frustration and the desperate need to feel 'even' with the market. The 6% Rule's mandatory trading halt is Elder's mechanical intervention against revenge trading.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder use the scuba diving analogy for psychological self-management?
Answers: A professional scuba diver monitors their air supply with constant vigilance, knowing that carelessness means death. Elder uses this analogy to describe the psychological discipline of monitoring the trading account. The money in the account is the trader's air supply; every careless expenditure brings them closer to the surface — and a depleted account means no more trading. This metaphor grounds abstract psychological principles in a visceral, survival-oriented image.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological state of a trader who is 'in the zone'?
Answers: A trader 'in the zone' operates from a state of calm, rule-based engagement where emotional noise has been minimised and analytical judgment is unimpaired. Elder describes a professional trader from Texas who could trade without anyone being able to tell from watching whether he was $2,000 ahead or behind — because winning did not elate him and losing did not deflate him. This equanimity is the psychological target state Elder describes as professional.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological importance of pre-market preparation?
Answers: Pre-market preparation — completing the Triple Screen analysis, identifying entry and exit levels, and calculating position sizes before markets open — is Elder's primary tool for managing psychology during trading hours. By making all analytical decisions when calm and objective, the trader converts intraday execution from a series of high-stakes real-time judgments into the mechanical implementation of a previously decided plan.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is Elder's advice for traders who feel psychologically impaired or distracted?
Answers: Elder explicitly states that traders should not trade when under unusual personal, emotional, or physical stress. Trading while distracted by personal problems, illness, or extreme fatigue introduces psychological impairment into the decision-making process. The correct response is to reduce size to minimum or stand aside entirely until normal psychological function is restored.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder use the 'writing down reasons for entering and exiting' as a psychological tool?
Answers: Writing the reasons for every trade decision before it is made serves multiple psychological functions: it forces articulation of the rationale (exposing vague or impulsive logic); it creates a pre-committed benchmark that the trade can be held accountable to; and it prevents post-hoc rationalisation by establishing the contemporaneous reasoning before the outcome is known.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – Trading for a Living (community reviews & quotes) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/104311.Trading_for_a_Living

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of 'not changing the plan while in an open position'?
Answers: Elder states this as a cardinal rule: you must not change your plan while you have an open position. The moment a position is open, the trader's objectivity is gone — every market movement is experienced as personally directed at their position, and the mind generates rationalisations for abandoning the original plan. All plan changes must therefore be made either before entry or after exit, when emotional involvement is minimal.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – Trading for a Living (community reviews & quotes) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/104311.Trading_for_a_Living

Questions: What is the psychological significance of Elder's statement that 'you can never control the market but you can learn to control yourself'?
Answers: This statement is Elder's core psychological teaching in a single sentence. Attempting to control markets — through wishing, forecasting, praying, or adding to a losing position — is psychologically exhausting and practically futile. Redirecting that energy toward controlling one's own behaviour — following rules, managing emotions, maintaining discipline — is both achievable and the actual determinant of trading success.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – Trading for a Living (community reviews & quotes) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/104311.Trading_for_a_Living

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological relationship between patience and profitability?
Answers: Elder identifies impatience — trading when there is no clear signal, or rushing entry before all three screens align — as a major source of unnecessary losses. Psychological patience means the willingness to sit in cash, doing nothing, until the market offers a genuinely high-probability setup. He points out that an astute trader enters the market during quiet times and takes profits during wild times — the psychological opposite of how amateurs behave.
Label: Psychology
Source: Medium / Coinmonks – Trading for a Living by Alexander Elder | https://medium.com/coinmonks/trading-for-a-living-by-alexander-elder-fddd428eca5a

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of 'watching dollars' versus 'watching indicators'?
Answers: Elder recommends that during trading hours, the trader should focus on prices and indicators rather than on the dollar value of their account or what they could buy with the profits. Watching the dollar fluctuation of an open position triggers emotional responses — greed when it rises, fear when it falls — that impair analytical judgment. Focusing on the indicator signals maintains the analytical frame that the trading plan requires.
Label: Psychology
Source: Medium / Coinmonks – Trading for a Living by Alexander Elder | https://medium.com/coinmonks/trading-for-a-living-by-alexander-elder-fddd428eca5a

Questions: How does Elder describe the psychology of focusing on process rather than outcome?
Answers: Elder teaches that 'the goal of a good trader is not to make money but to trade well.' This reframing of success from financial outcome to process quality has profound psychological benefits: it makes each trade evaluable on its own terms (was the plan followed correctly?) rather than on the unpredictable result; it enables learning from every trade regardless of P&L; and it reduces the emotional swings that outcome-focused trading creates.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the difference between 'bending rules' and 'breaking them'?
Answers: Elder views all rule violations as rule breaks — there is no psychologically safe 'bending.' Every deviation from the plan, even once, demonstrates to the trader's own mind that the rules are negotiable. This demonstration makes future violations more likely, gradually eroding the entire framework of discipline. The professional treats their rules as absolute because they understand that partial compliance produces the same psychological degradation as complete non-compliance.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder recommend a trader do immediately after a large loss?
Answers: After a large loss, Elder recommends: (1) stopping trading for the rest of the session; (2) reviewing the trade journal to identify what rule was violated or what psychological state caused the deviation; (3) reducing position size for the next period as a concrete act of self-imposed accountability; and (4) using the experience to update the journal with a specific corrective intention rather than vague promises to 'do better.'
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological danger of a 'hot streak'?
Answers: A hot streak — multiple consecutive winning trades — is paradoxically one of the most dangerous psychological states in trading. It creates the powerful illusion of invincibility, induces rule relaxation ('this trade is so obvious I don't need to size it at 2%'), and sets up the inevitable return to the mean in a psychologically vulnerable, over-exposed position. Elder treats the end of a hot streak with the same caution as the start of a losing one.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of a trader who keeps 'running from failures'?
Answers: A trader who constantly changes systems, markets, and mentors after each failure is running from self-knowledge. The different systems and markets provide new environments for the same psychological patterns to play out again. Elder teaches that genuine improvement requires staying long enough with a system and a journal to accumulate the self-knowledge that enables real change — not escaping the discomfort of failure by seeking a new external fix.
Label: Psychology
Source: Bookey – Trading for a Living Chapter Summary | https://www.bookey.app/book/trading-for-a-living

Questions: What does Elder say about the role of physical health in trading psychology?
Answers: Elder notes that physical stress, illness, fatigue, and poor health impair the cognitive and emotional regulation capacities that professional trading requires. He recommends that traders pay attention to their physical state before each session: sleep quality, energy level, and freedom from illness are all factors that affect the quality of psychological control a trader can exercise during the trading day.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder's 'preaching is good for the preacher' comment relate to psychological development?
Answers: Elder states that showing chart patterns and stressing the need for discipline reinforces his own performance. He notes that to master a concept you have to be able to teach it — this acknowledgement captures how articulating and teaching psychological principles serves the teacher's own psychological maintenance, keeping them active rather than allowing them to fade into habit.
Label: Psychology
Source: Amazon – The New Trading for a Living | https://www.amazon.com/New-Trading-Living-Psychology-Discipline/dp/1118443926

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of 'a trader who cannot explain what they know'?
Answers: Elder writes: 'To master a concept you have to be able to teach it to others. If you can't explain what you think you know, then you really don't know it.' This applies to both technical and psychological knowledge. A trader who cannot clearly articulate their rules, their process, and their psychological vulnerabilities has not truly internalised them — and will revert to unconscious emotional behaviour under pressure.
Label: Psychology
Source: Amazon – The New Trading for a Living | https://www.amazon.com/New-Trading-Living-Psychology-Discipline/dp/1118443926

Questions: What does Elder say is the primary psychological difference between winners and losers?
Answers: Winners approach trading from the perspective of a mental discipline: they have clear rules, they follow them consistently, and they do not let outcomes affect their adherence to the process. Losers approach trading from an emotional orientation: they react to market moves, let fear and greed override their plans, and spend their energy looking for external explanations for their failures rather than internal ones.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – Trading for a Living (community reviews & quotes) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/104311.Trading_for_a_Living

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of 'getting even' with the market?
Answers: The impulse to 'get even' — to recover a loss by immediately trading again with larger size — is one of the most destructive psychological patterns Elder identifies. It treats the market as a personal adversary who has wronged the trader, rather than as an impersonal process that executed a predetermined outcome. This personalisation leads to revenge trades made under emotional impairment, typically amplifying the original loss.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of losing traders who 'trade in a fog'?
Answers: Elder writes that a loser 'trades in a fog and doesn't know why he keeps losing.' If the loser knew the reason for their losses, they would have corrected it and become a winner. The fog is created by denial, selective memory, and the refusal to keep accurate records. The journal is Elder's prescription for clearing this fog: it makes the patterns of losing behaviour visible and undeniable.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What six psychological rules for beginning traders does Elder recommend?
Answers: Elder's six starting rules are: (1) commit to trading for the long haul; (2) learn continuously but maintain healthy scepticism; (3) do not rush to trade — take time to learn; (4) develop a systematic analytical method; (5) develop a money management plan; (6) be aware that you are your own worst enemy through greed, fear, and emotions.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – Trading for a Living (community reviews & quotes) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/104311.Trading_for_a_Living

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological importance of 'healthy scepticism'?
Answers: Healthy scepticism means testing every claimed edge or system against your own data rather than accepting it on faith. Psychologically, this protects the trader from the guru trap — the surrender of independent judgment to an authority. Elder recommends adopting a scientist's disposition: form a hypothesis, test it rigorously, and accept or reject it based on evidence rather than on the persuasiveness of the person presenting it.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – Trading for a Living (community reviews & quotes) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/104311.Trading_for_a_Living

Questions: How does Elder describe the psychological difference between a long-haul trader and a short-term speculator?
Answers: The long-haul trader has made a psychological commitment to remain in the market through bad periods, continuing to learn and refine their process over years. The short-term speculator treats each losing stretch as a potential reason to quit, which prevents the sustained experience and self-knowledge development that profitable trading ultimately requires. The commitment to the long haul is as much a psychological prerequisite as any specific trading skill.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – Trading for a Living (community reviews & quotes) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/104311.Trading_for_a_Living

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of the 'equity curve' as a self-management tool?
Answers: The personal equity curve plots account value over time as an objective record of cumulative trading performance. A rising equity curve confirms that the system and psychological execution are working; a declining curve is objective evidence of a problem that must be diagnosed before trading continues. The equity curve cannot be argued with or rationalised — it reflects the sum of all decisions made, emotional and analytical.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What psychological insight does Elder share about 'an astute trader entering during quiet times'?
Answers: Elder observes that astute traders enter the market during quiet times — when the setup is ripe but excitement is low — and take profits during wild times — when the crowd is emotionally extreme and prices are extended. Amateurs do exactly the opposite: they rush in when activity is high (driven by FOMO) and stay out when things are quiet (driven by boredom). This psychological inversion of optimal timing is a direct consequence of emotional rather than analytical decision-making.
Label: Psychology
Source: Medium / Coinmonks – Trading for a Living by Alexander Elder | https://medium.com/coinmonks/trading-for-a-living-by-alexander-elder-fddd428eca5a

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological significance of 'keeping good records'?
Answers: Elder repeatedly states: 'Show me a trader with good records and I'll show you a good trader.' Good records represent a form of psychological accountability — the willingness to confront the unvarnished reality of one's own performance. Traders who avoid keeping records are, in Elder's framework, exhibiting the same denial as the alcoholic who doesn't want to count their drinks.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological danger of not having a trading plan?
Answers: Without a written trading plan, every trade becomes a real-time emotional decision made under the influence of market noise, recent outcomes, and mood. Elder compares this to driving without a map or a destination — the trader wanders wherever the market's current emotional tide takes them. The plan converts the session from an emotional improvisation into the execution of a previously considered strategy.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of 'learning from mistakes'?
Answers: Elder's rule is: 'It is OK to make mistakes, but not OK to repeat them.' This framing removes the shame from mistakes (they are inevitable in learning) while firmly targeting their root cause (failure to understand what went wrong). The journal is the tool that converts each mistake into a learning event — by documenting the reasoning and emotional state that produced it, enabling conscious recognition and correction.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder describe the psychological transformation required to become a 'professional' trader?
Answers: Becoming a professional trader requires a psychological transformation in which the trader transitions from outcome-orientation (winning money) to process-orientation (trading correctly). This shift is more fundamental than any technical skill acquisition — it involves a reorganisation of what 'success' means in trading, what emotional experiences are normal and acceptable, and how failures are processed and used.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of 'setting realistic goals'?
Answers: Unrealistic goals — 'I will double my account this year' — create psychological pressure that distorts decision-making.
