When progress falls behind an ambitious target, the trader is tempted to increase risk to 'catch up,' producing the very losses that move them further from the goal. Elder recommends setting process goals (maintain 2% maximum risk on every trade; review the journal after every session) rather than financial goals, which are partially outside the trader's control.
Label: Psychology
Source: Bookey – Trading for a Living Chapter Summary | https://www.bookey.app/book/trading-for-a-living

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological value of trading smaller size during learning?
Answers: Trading minimum position size while learning — the recommendation Elder makes to beginners — is primarily a psychological prescription. Small size keeps losses financially trivial, enabling the trader to learn from mistakes without the emotional overwhelm of significant financial pain. The fear and greed that distort decision-making are calibrated to dollar amounts; trade small and the emotions remain manageable enough to observe and learn from.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological challenges unique to trading versus other professions?
Answers: Unlike virtually every other profession, trading provides no colleagues, no managers, and no clients to provide informal feedback, warnings, or accountability. The trader is entirely alone with their own psychological vulnerabilities in an environment designed to exploit them. Elder argues this makes trading arguably the most demanding psychological challenge among all human activities — which is why dedicated psychological self-management is not optional but essential.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological role of the trading community in development?
Answers: Elder founded SpikeTrade and the Traders' Camps specifically because he recognised that community provides the psychological scaffolding that solo trading lacks. Sharing trades, receiving feedback, and being accountable to peers replaces some of the natural accountability structures of other professions. Community membership also combats the isolation-driven irrationality that makes solo trading psychologically vulnerable.
Label: Psychology
Source: Elder.com – About Dr. Elder | https://www.elder.com/about

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of market 'noise' versus meaningful signals?
Answers: Market noise consists of random short-term price fluctuations that are psychologically compelling — they look like signals — but carry no useful information. The psychological challenge is distinguishing between noise and genuine trend: beginners react to noise constantly (overtrading); professionals filter out noise by using longer timeframes and multiple confirming indicators. Developing psychological tolerance for short-term randomness — resisting the urge to react to every tick — is a major element of trading maturity.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder describe the 'inner war' between discipline and impulse?
Answers: Elder identifies an ongoing inner war in every trader between the disciplined, rule-following part of the mind and the impulsive, emotion-driven part. Rules are the disciplined part's attempt to bind the impulsive part in advance; the journal's honest review is the mechanism for understanding which part won each session and why. Professional trading is not the absence of impulse — it is the systematic victory of discipline over impulse through pre-committed rules.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological value of 'having a good system you believe in'?
Answers: Psychological conviction in a trading system is necessary for disciplined execution. A trader who doubts their system will deviate from it at the first challenge, abandoning entries at the critical moment or exiting at the first adverse tick. Building conviction through honest backtesting, paper trading, and careful real-money tracking creates the psychological stability needed to follow the system through normal drawdown periods without abandoning it.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of 'trading against strong trends'?
Answers: Attempting to pick tops and bottoms — trading against strong trends — is a psychologically driven error. The trader who 'knows' that the market is overvalued feels compelled to short it before the objective technical evidence of reversal appears. This premature counter-trend positioning combines intellectual arrogance (the belief that the trader's valuation is more reliable than the market's current consensus) with the self-destructive impulse to be 'right' rather than profitable.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of 'analysis paralysis'?
Answers: Analysis paralysis — the inability to act despite clear signals because of excessive doubt — is the opposite of overconfidence but equally destructive. It is usually caused by a previous loss that has made the trader afraid of committing to any position. Elder's prescription is the same as for overconfidence: return to the system, follow the rules, and use position size as a dial — size down (rather than not trading at all) when confidence is low.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What psychological advice does Elder give for managing the 'fear of missing out' (FOMO)?
Answers: FOMO — the fear of missing a move and rushing into entry after a breakout — is the emotional drive behind one of the most common timing errors. Elder's prescription is to pre-define the entry criteria in the trading plan and stand aside if the conditions are not met. Another opportunity will always follow; a bad entry made out of FOMO typically produces either a loss or a worse-than-planned risk-reward ratio.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of holding a winner too long?
Answers: Holding a winner too long is driven by greed — the unwillingness to accept a specific, limited profit when a larger, unlimited one seems possible. Elder addresses this through pre-defined profit targets (the oscillator reaching overbought, the upper channel line) that convert the exit decision from a real-time emotional choice into the execution of a pre-committed plan. The 'enough is the power word' principle is the psychological antidote to greed-driven overholding.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is Elder's psychological prescription for rebuilding after a period of consistent losses?
Answers: Elder recommends four steps: (1) stop trading immediately to prevent further damage; (2) conduct an honest journal review to identify the cause — system failure, execution failure, or changed market conditions; (3) return to paper trading or minimum size to rebuild confidence and test corrections; (4) when returning to real trading, do so at half the previous position size until the equity curve turns upward again.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of comparing yourself to other traders?
Answers: Comparing performance to other traders is psychologically destructive because it substitutes a relative benchmark for the absolute benchmark of consistent rule-following. A trader who is losing money but losing less than peers has still failed psychologically by the standards of their own plan. Elder teaches traders to evaluate themselves only against their own standards — the question is not 'am I better than others?' but 'did I execute my plan correctly today?'
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the role of physical exercise and routine in trader psychology?
Answers: Elder advocates for traders to maintain regular physical exercise, consistent sleep schedules, and structured daily routines as part of their psychological self-management practice. Physical regulation supports emotional regulation: a trader who is rested, physically active, and living according to a consistent daily structure has the neurological resources for the disciplined, calm, and objective decision-making that profitable trading requires.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say is the psychological significance of the Kerry Lovvorn quote he frequently cites?
Answers: Elder's partner Kerry Lovvorn is quoted as saying: 'It is hard enough to know what the market is going to do; if you don't know what you are going to do, the game is lost.' Elder uses this to emphasise that trading preparation is as much about knowing your own planned responses as it is about knowing market analysis. Self-knowledge — what you will do when the signal appears, when the stop is hit, when a large loss occurs — is the complement to market knowledge.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the long-term psychological journey of becoming a professional trader?
Answers: Elder describes the journey as an ongoing practice rather than a destination: there is no point at which the psychological work is complete. Each market cycle brings new challenges; the psychological habits built during calm periods are tested during crises. The professional trader commits to lifelong psychological self-management — maintaining the journal, following the rules, reviewing the equity curve, and treating each day as an opportunity to execute the system correctly regardless of recent outcomes.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of 'being your own teacher'?
Answers: Elder's ultimate psychological goal for the trader is self-sufficiency: becoming your own teacher through honest record review. No external mentor can be present for every trade; only the trader's own journal and equity curve provide the complete, unfiltered record needed for genuine self-instruction. The discipline to maintain and honestly review those records is the psychological practice that produces compounding improvement.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological impact of social media tips and trading chat rooms?
Answers: Elder warns that consuming other traders' opinions — from newsletters, chat rooms, or gurus — exposes the trader to psychological contamination: borrowed biases, contagious excitement, and misaligned timeframes. The professional isolates themselves during analysis to ensure their market read reflects their own system's signals, not the prevailing crowd sentiment on social media.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of trading during personal stress?
Answers: Elder advises traders not to trade when under significant personal stress — relationship problems, health concerns, financial pressures outside trading — because stress depletes the psychological resources needed for disciplined execution. The brain under stress defaults to impulsive, short-term focused behaviour that is incompatible with the patient, rule-following approach professional trading requires.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder describe the psychology of a good trade that loses money?
Answers: A good trade is one executed according to all the rules — correct setup identification, appropriate position sizing, pre-set stops and targets, no mid-trade rule violations — even if it ultimately loses money. The outcome of any individual trade is partly random; the quality of the process is entirely within the trader's control. Elder evaluates himself by process quality, not trade outcomes.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What is the psychological significance of the trade grade system in Elder's framework?
Answers: The trade grade (Buy Grade, Sell Grade, Trade Grade) shifts performance evaluation from outcome (did I make money?) to process quality (did I execute well?). This is a profound psychological reframing: it rewards the discipline of buying near support and selling near resistance regardless of whether the overall trade was profitable, making excellent execution reinforcing even in losing trades.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about psychological readiness before placing a trade?
Answers: Elder recommends a readiness-to-trade self-assessment before each session — evaluating emotional state, clarity of analysis, physical wellbeing, and confidence in the system. Entering a trade while not psychologically ready produces the same impaired execution as entering without a technical signal. The readiness assessment is a pre-commitment tool that reduces the probability of emotionally compromised trading.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological trap of confirmation bias in market analysis?
Answers: Confirmation bias — noticing evidence that supports an existing belief while discounting contradicting evidence — is particularly dangerous in trading because the financial stake amplifies the bias. Elder's countermeasure is to write specific entry criteria before looking at the chart, then check objectively whether those criteria are met rather than scanning for confirmation of a pre-existing view.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological benefit of having a written trading plan?
Answers: A written trading plan provides a psychological anchor that resists real-time emotional distortion. During market hours, when prices are moving and emotions are engaged, the written plan represents the trader's best pre-market judgment — made when calm and objective. Consulting the plan during trading anchors decisions in that rational state rather than in the current emotional one.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological state at market tops and bottoms?
Answers: At market tops, crowd psychology reaches maximum optimism — news is universally positive and sceptics are mocked. At bottoms, crowd psychology reaches maximum pessimism — news is universally negative and optimists are ridiculed. Elder teaches that these extreme consensus states are psychological reversal indicators: the moment when crowd emotion is most uniform is typically just before the trend reverses.
Label: Psychology
Source: ACY Securities – Mass Psychology: Why Price Moves the Way It Does | https://acy.com/en/market-news/education/mass-psychology-why-price-moves-the-way-it-does-j-o-20251226-105138/

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology behind support and resistance levels?
Answers: Support and resistance are created by the memories and pain of market participants. Traders who bought at a prior support level feel relief when price returns there and sell to exit at breakeven, creating selling pressure. Traders who missed a prior rally feel regret and buy eagerly when price returns to that level, creating buying pressure. These psychological responses make certain price levels sticky regardless of fundamental value.
Label: Psychology
Source: AllBookStores – The New Trading for a Living (reviews) | https://www.allbookstores.com/The-New-Trading-Living-Psychology/9781118443927

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of price as consensus?
Answers: Every price at which a trade occurs is the consensus of a willing buyer and seller at that moment — psychologically driven, not mathematically determined. This consensus changes constantly as the crowd's emotional state evolves. Elder's indicators track the direction and intensity of these consensus shifts, making technical analysis a real-time measurement of crowd psychology.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – Trading for a Living (community reviews & quotes) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/104311.Trading_for_a_Living

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological nature of a market trend?
Answers: A trend is a sustained psychological consensus: buyers consistently more motivated than sellers over time. Trends are born in uncertainty when the new consensus is forming, develop as the crowd builds confidence, and die in euphoria when everyone is convinced the trend will continue forever. The MACD histogram's declining slope identifies the moment when the dominant group's strength is fading.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of herd mentality in financial markets?
Answers: Herd mentality in markets is the tendency to abandon individual analysis and follow the perceived crowd consensus — buying because others are buying, selling because others are selling. Elder identifies herd behaviour as the root cause of both bubble formation and panic selling. The technical trader who uses oscillators to identify overbought and oversold crowd extremes has a structural advantage over herd participants.
Label: Psychology
Source: ACY Securities – Mass Psychology: Why Price Moves the Way It Does | https://acy.com/en/market-news/education/mass-psychology-why-price-moves-the-way-it-does-j-o-20251226-105138/

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of support turning into resistance?
Answers: When a support level is broken, the psychology of the market reverses. Traders who bought at that level are now holding losses and waiting for price to return so they can exit at breakeven. When price does return to the old support, these trapped holders sell, converting the former support into resistance. Elder uses this psychological mechanism to understand why support-turned-resistance is technically reliable.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology behind volume as a crowd indicator?
Answers: Elder teaches that volume reflects the intensity of the crowd's participation: high volume on an advance indicates enthusiastic buying — the crowd is strongly committed to the move. Low volume on an advance indicates lukewarm participation — the move lacks conviction and may be fragile. The Force Index explicitly incorporates this crowd-intensity reading into its calculation.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say is the psychological lesson from the crowd being stronger than you?
Answers: The practical implication of the crowd's superior strength is that fighting a strong trend — however compelling the analytical case for reversal — is psychologically and financially hazardous. The trader must confirm that the crowd's trend is actually exhausting through divergences and oscillator extremes before taking a counter-position. Premature counter-trend trading is a form of hubris the market reliably punishes.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – Trading for a Living (community reviews & quotes) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/104311.Trading_for_a_Living

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological significance of the phrase the market is not your mother?
Answers: Elder uses this statement to confront the fantasy that the market is a benevolent provider. The market is adversarial: every other participant is trying to take your money, not provide it. Recognising this reality eliminates the passive, entitled psychological stance that leads traders to sit in losing positions waiting for the market to come back and make them whole.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of good psychiatry and good trading having one principle in common?
Answers: Elder identifies the shared core principle as a commitment to reality: both good psychiatry and good trading require seeing the world as it actually is — resisting the human tendency to distort, deny, or wish away uncomfortable facts. A psychiatrist who sees patients through their own biases cannot help them; a trader who reads charts through the filter of their wishes cannot profit from them.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological significance of one day at a time for a recovering trader?
Answers: For a trader rebuilding after loss and undisciplined trading, one day at a time means focusing exclusively on executing rules correctly in today's session — not worrying about recovering the past drawdown or achieving future targets. Each disciplined day builds the habit of compliance and the confidence that the system works, which are prerequisites for the gradual recovery of both financial and psychological capital.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of self-knowledge as input into system design?
Answers: A trader's system should be designed to complement their psychological strengths and compensate for weaknesses. A trader prone to overtrading needs strict entry criteria that filter out most signals. A trader prone to cutting winners early needs mechanical trailing stops that remove the exit decision from real-time control. Self-knowledge is not just philosophically valuable — it is a practical input into building a system that the trader can actually follow.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder describe the psychological transformation experienced by his successful Traders Camp participants?
Answers: Elder observes that Traders Camp participants who truly change are those who, for the first time, take full personal responsibility for their trading results. The psychological shift from external blame to internal ownership is the pivot around which genuine improvement becomes possible. Without this shift, technical education produces no lasting benefit.
Label: Psychology
Source: Elder.com – About Dr. Elder | https://www.elder.com/about

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological danger of not having a trading plan?
Answers: Without a written trading plan, every trade becomes a real-time emotional decision made under the influence of market noise, recent outcomes, and mood. Elder compares this to driving without a map or a destination — the trader wanders wherever the market's current emotional tide takes them. The plan converts the session from an emotional improvisation into the execution of a previously considered strategy.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of accepting uncertainty in markets?
Answers: Accepting uncertainty — the willingness to make probabilistic decisions without needing to know the outcome — is a fundamental psychological prerequisite for trading. Traders who cannot tolerate uncertainty seek certainty through prediction, guru-following, or holding losers hoping for reversal, all of which produce poor outcomes. The professional's task is to make high-quality decisions under uncertainty, not to eliminate uncertainty.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological function of the 2% Rule for mental wellbeing?
Answers: The 2% Rule has a direct psychological benefit beyond risk management: it eliminates catastrophic losses, which are the primary cause of the desperate psychological states — panic, shame, revenge trading — that impair further decision-making. By capping any individual loss at a level the trader can absorb without overwhelming emotional reaction, the 2% Rule preserves the psychological stability needed to continue trading rationally.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of being in the market for the long haul?
Answers: The commitment to trading for the long haul — to still being a trader in 20 years — is a psychological reframing that transforms every loss from a potential exit event into a tuition payment. This long-term orientation makes discipline easier because the individual trade matters less. The short-term oriented trader, by contrast, experiences every loss as a crisis that threatens their entire trading career.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – Trading for a Living (community reviews & quotes) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/104311.Trading_for_a_Living

Questions: What does Elder say about focusing on the process rather than the profit?
Answers: Focusing on the process rather than the profit eliminates the emotional distortions that outcome-orientation creates. When the trader's satisfaction comes from correct process execution rather than from the dollar result, fear and greed lose their power to distort decisions. Each correctly executed trade is a success regardless of P&L; each rule violation is a failure regardless of outcome.
Label: Psychology
Source: Medium / Coinmonks – Trading for a Living by Alexander Elder | https://medium.com/coinmonks/trading-for-a-living-by-alexander-elder-fddd428eca5a

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of the technical analysis as applied social psychology?
Answers: Elder teaches that every technical indicator measures some aspect of crowd behaviour. The EMA measures the crowd's average opinion of value; the MACD histogram measures momentum in the crowd's sentiment; oscillators measure emotional extremes. Understanding the psychological meaning behind each indicator makes the trader a reader of human behaviour rather than a mechanical follower of mathematical signals.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder describe the psychology behind crowds creating their own leaders through price?
Answers: Elder writes that crowd members remain unaware that by focusing on price they create their own leader. As traders collectively watch the same price level, their collective reaction to it — buying at support, selling at resistance — produces the very price behaviours they were watching for, reinforcing their belief in the pattern. The crowd self-fulfils its own expectations through this collective psychological dynamic.
Label: Psychology
Source: Medium / Coinmonks – Trading for a Living by Alexander Elder | https://medium.com/coinmonks/trading-for-a-living-by-alexander-elder-fddd428eca5a

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of winners feeling rewarded and losers feeling punished?
Answers: Winners feel rewarded when price moves in their favour and losers feel punished when it moves against them. This operant conditioning produces irrational behaviour: winners become overconfident and take excessive risks; losers become anxious and exit prematurely or double down. Professionals insulate their decisions from this reward-punishment cycle by focusing on process rather than real-time P&L.
Label: Psychology
Source: Medium / Coinmonks – Trading for a Living by Alexander Elder | https://medium.com/coinmonks/trading-for-a-living-by-alexander-elder-fddd428eca5a

Questions: What is the most important psychological insight in Elder's entire body of work?
Answers: The most important psychological insight in Elder's work is: you can never control the market, but you can learn to control yourself. All of Elder's technical tools, risk rules, and systems are secondary to this insight. A trader who masters self-control can adapt any system to profit consistently; a trader who lacks self-control will destroy even the most technically sound system. The locus of trading success is internal, not external.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – Trading for a Living (community reviews & quotes) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/104311.Trading_for_a_Living

Questions: What does Elder say is the psychological reward of mastering trading?
Answers: Elder writes that trading mastered is the ultimate expression of personal freedom: the trader can live and work anywhere in the world, be independent from routine, and answer to no one. But he is clear that this freedom is earned only through the most demanding psychological work — the disciplined confrontation of one's own fears, impulses, and self-deceptions over years of honest self-examination. The freedom is the reward of the psychological mastery, not its precondition.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder summarise the psychological journey from amateur to professional trader?
Answers: The journey from amateur to professional is fundamentally psychological. The amateur chases profits, avoids acknowledging mistakes, externalises blame, and trades impulsively. The professional manages a process, documents everything honestly, accepts full personal responsibility, and follows rules consistently regardless of short-term outcomes. The technical skills required take months to learn; the psychological transformation takes years of honest self-examination and deliberate practice.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the relationship between psychology and the Three Ms in his final summary?
Answers: Elder's final synthesis is that Mind, Method, and Money are three expressions of the same underlying requirement: a disciplined, realistic, and rules-based approach to trading. The Mind provides the psychological stability to follow rules under pressure; the Method provides the rules; Money management provides the structure that makes following the rules financially survivable. Psychology is not a separate component — it is the capacity that makes all the others functional.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological significance of the daily trading commitment?
Answers: Elder emphasises that the psychological battle of trading is won or lost one day at a time. Each session represents a fresh choice: to follow the rules or to deviate; to maintain the journal or to skip it; to stand aside without a signal or to trade out of boredom. The accumulated result of these daily choices — not any single brilliant trade — determines whether a trader becomes professionally profitable over time.
Label: Psychology
Source: Bookey – Trading for a Living Chapter Summary | https://www.bookey.app/book/trading-for-a-living

Questions: What does Elder say about the ultimate psychological prerequisite for trading success?
Answers: Elder summarises the ultimate psychological prerequisite as the ability to confront reality without flinching — to see the market as it is rather than as you wish it to be, to see yourself as you are rather than as you wish to be, and to act on that honest assessment rather than on hope or rationalisation. This fundamental commitment to reality over fantasy is the psychological foundation upon which all technical skill and risk management must be built.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological challenge of knowing when not to trade?
Answers: Knowing when not to trade — standing aside when the setup does not meet all criteria — is psychologically harder than placing trades, because it requires tolerating boredom and the fear of missing a move. Elder teaches that the willingness to do nothing when the setup is absent is the hallmark of mature trading — the market always provides the next opportunity for the patient and disciplined trader.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the long-term compounding effect of psychological self-improvement?
Answers: Each disciplined session that follows the plan builds habits that make the next disciplined session easier. Each journal review that honestly identifies a psychological error makes that error slightly less likely in future. Over months and years, these small incremental improvements in psychological performance compound into a fundamentally more disciplined trader — which Elder considers the primary variable in long-run profitability.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of not focusing on dollar P&L during trading hours?
Answers: Elder recommends focusing on prices and indicator signals rather than on the dollar value of an open position. Watching dollar swings triggers greed when the position is up and fear when it is down. Focusing on whether the system conditions are still met maintains the analytical frame that the trading plan requires, insulating decisions from the emotional reaction to nominal gains and losses.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological role of backtesting in building trading conviction?
Answers: Backtesting builds psychological conviction by providing objective evidence that the system has a positive expectancy over historical data. This evidence-based confidence is the healthy foundation for disciplined execution: the trader follows the system not out of blind faith but because they have empirically verified that it works over time.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of physical health and trading performance?
Answers: Elder notes that physical stress, illness, fatigue, and poor sleep impair the cognitive and emotional regulation capacities that professional trading requires. He recommends that traders pay attention to their physical state before each session: sleep quality, energy level, and freedom from illness all affect the quality of psychological control a trader can exercise.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological danger of emotional contagion from financial news media?
Answers: Financial news media is designed to provoke emotional reactions — fear, excitement, urgency — that drive viewership. Responding to headlines with position changes introduces emotional contagion that overrides the trader's own system. The disciplined practice is to evaluate media content against the system's criteria before acting, not to trade the emotional response the headline provokes.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about trading with money you cannot afford to lose?
Answers: Trading with money needed for living expenses creates an environment in which every adverse market movement is experienced as a threat to survival. Under this psychological pressure, rational decision-making is impossible. Elder is categorical: a trader must use only risk capital — money whose loss would not threaten their financial security — to maintain the emotional equilibrium sound trading requires.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of sticking to a system through normal drawdown periods?
Answers: Every profitable system experiences drawdown periods — consecutive losses that are statistically normal but psychologically painful. The trader who abandons their system during normal drawdown loses both the potential recovery and the accumulated evidence about the system's behaviour. Psychological conviction in a system, built through honest backtesting, is what enables the trader to hold through drawdowns without abandoning the approach.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of market entry versus market exit decisions?
Answers: Elder observes that most traders invest far more psychological energy in finding entries than in planning exits. Yet exits determine the actual profit or loss on every trade. He teaches that equal psychological attention must be given to both sides: the entry plan identifies the opportunity; the exit plan determines whether the opportunity is realised or squandered.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological function of trading rules in constraining the ego?
Answers: Trading rules serve as a system of checks on the ego's two most destructive tendencies: overconfidence (ignoring risk limits because this trade is certain) and denial (refusing to exit a loser because the trade cannot be wrong). Rules are pre-commitments made when the ego was calm and objective; they protect the trading account from the ego's distorted real-time judgments.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological trap of needing to be right?
Answers: The need to be right causes traders to hold losers indefinitely, to add to losing positions hoping the market will eventually validate their analysis, and to exit winners early to secure proof that they were correct before the trade can turn against them. Elder teaches that the professional measures success by process quality — not by the market confirming their personal view.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of overtrading and its emotional roots?
Answers: Overtrading — placing far more trades than the system justifies — is driven by the emotional need for action: excitement, the thrill of participation, and the inability to sit in cash. Elder identifies this as a gambling impulse masquerading as trading activity. The system's strict entry criteria define when trading is analytical versus emotional.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder describe the psychological parallel between trading discipline and athletic training?
Answers: Just as an elite athlete builds muscle memory through thousands of deliberate repetitions, a trader builds decision-making discipline through hundreds of carefully reviewed trades. The journal review is the trader's film study session; the trade plan is the game plan; the rules are the training protocol. Excellence in both fields is the product of systematic, honest, and persistent deliberate practice.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about looking for the best and safest trades each session?
Answers: Elder's goal for every session is not to trade as much as possible but to find the best and safest trades — those where the setup fully meets all three screens' criteria, the risk-reward is favourable, and position sizing is within the Iron Triangle limits. This quality-over-quantity psychological orientation automatically filters out impulsive, marginal trades that drain accounts through small recurring losses.
Label: Psychology
Source: Collin Seow – Book Review: Trading for a Living (Part 1) | https://www.collinseow.com/book-review-trading-for-living-1/

Questions: What does Elder say about staying serene during a winning streak?
Answers: Serenity during a winning streak requires the same psychological discipline as serenity during a losing one. The winning streak's temptation is to conclude that the rules can be relaxed — that the trader's skill has transcended the need for the system's constraints. Elder teaches that this conclusion is always false: the rules that produced the winning streak are precisely the ones that must be maintained to sustain it.
Label: Psychology
Source: Collin Seow – Book Review: Trading for a Living (Part 1) | https://www.collinseow.com/book-review-trading-for-living-1/

Questions: What is Elder's psychological teaching for managing the transition from a losing period to recovery?
Answers: The key teaching for recovery is: slow down, shrink your size, and return to fundamentals before accelerating again. The psychological impulse during recovery is to trade aggressively to catch up. Elder teaches the opposite: treat recovery as a new beginning at minimum size, prove that the rules are being followed correctly, and let the equity curve's upward movement — not the desire to recover losses quickly — determine when size can be incrementally restored.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about using the journal to identify recurring psychological mistakes?
Answers: The journal's most powerful function is identifying recurring patterns: the trader who consistently exits winners before the target reveals a fear-of-reversal pattern; the trader who consistently holds losers past the stop reveals a denial pattern. Only by documenting every trade honestly and reviewing the accumulated evidence can these patterns be seen and deliberately corrected.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of chart patterns repeating across time?
Answers: Chart patterns repeat not because of mathematical laws but because the psychological situations that produce them recur: crowds consistently panic at the same types of levels, consistently become euphoric at the same types of breakouts, and consistently feel the same regret and relief at prior highs and lows. Understanding this psychological foundation makes pattern recognition more reliable.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder use the phrase the weakest link to motivate psychological development?
Answers: By identifying the trader as the weakest link in any trading system, Elder motivates systematic psychological self-improvement. The technical system and the risk rules are already well-designed; what remains is the human execution component. Improving the weakest link means improving psychological self-management, which has more impact on overall performance than any additional indicator or system refinement.
Label: Psychology
Source: Goodreads – Trading for a Living (community reviews & quotes) | https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/104311.Trading_for_a_Living

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of accepting that trading is a minus-sum game?
Answers: Accepting that trading is a minus-sum game — where the industry drains money through commissions and slippage — has an important psychological implication: being simply better than average is not enough. The trader must be psychologically rigorous enough to be significantly above average to overcome the structural disadvantage. This acceptance of the full difficulty of the challenge is itself a form of the clear-eyed psychology Elder advocates throughout.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology behind developing patience as a tradeable skill?
Answers: Patience in trading is not passive waiting — it is the active psychological practice of resisting the urge to act in the absence of a valid signal. Developing patience means repeatedly experiencing the discomfort of sitting in cash and consciously choosing rule compliance over action. Over hundreds of sessions, this transforms impatience from a recurring impulse into a recognised temptation that can be deliberately resisted.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of comparing actual trades to the original plan?
Answers: After every trade, Elder recommends comparing the actual execution to the plan: was the entry at the planned level? Was the stop placed correctly? Was the exit taken at the right signal? This comparison — requiring honesty — identifies the gap between the trader's rules and their actual behaviour. Closing this gap is what psychological improvement means in Elder's framework.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of commitment versus stubbornness in holding positions?
Answers: Commitment means holding because the system's conditions for holding still apply. Stubbornness means holding because the trader is unwilling to admit they were wrong. The committed trader has a stop and a plan; the stubborn trader has hope. Elder's pre-set stop orders convert the commitment/stubbornness decision into something structural — the stop was placed before entry and cannot be rationalised away in real time.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of fear as the flip side of greed?
Answers: Fear and greed are twin destroyers. While greed drives overtrading, excessive size, and ignoring stop levels, fear causes premature exits, refusal to enter valid setups, and paralysis at critical moments. They are complementary emotional forces that disrupt trading from opposite directions. The antidote to both is identical: rule-following that removes the emotional decision from the moment of maximum psychological pressure.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of professionals having a statistical mindset?
Answers: A professional trader thinks statistically: they evaluate their system over hundreds of trades, accept that any individual trade outcome is partly random, and judge performance by the distribution of outcomes rather than by any single result. This statistical mindset enables them to take losses in stride — as expected elements of a profitable distribution — rather than experiencing each loss as a personal failure that demands immediate correction.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the long-term psychological significance of his daily trading activities?
Answers: Each trading day that Elder executes his system correctly — following all three screens, sizing within the Iron Triangle, maintaining the journal — constitutes a unit of psychological practice. Over years, these daily units of disciplined practice build the professional psychological architecture that makes profitable trading not just possible but reliable. The daily discipline is the practice; the long-term track record is the result.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychological impact of discussing trades with a mentor or peer?
Answers: Discussing trades with a mentor or trusted trading peer provides an external perspective that cuts through the self-deceptions the ego generates around every position. A peer can ask questions the trader has avoided asking themselves: why did you not take the stop? what made you increase size? These external questions are psychological mirrors that reflect the behaviour more accurately than private self-assessment.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of developing a personal trading identity?
Answers: Over time each trader must develop a personal trading identity — a clear sense of what kind of trader they are, what markets suit their temperament, and what their system's edge consists of. This identity provides psychological stability during adversity: the trader who knows who they are does not abandon their approach at the first challenge; they refine it.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of the Traders Camps in accelerating psychological development?
Answers: The Traders' Camps provide concentrated psychological development by immersing participants in a structured trading environment with direct feedback, shared journaling, and real accountability. Elder observes that breakthroughs — the moment when a trader genuinely internalises personal responsibility for their results — happen more frequently in the Camp environment than in solo trading, because the social context confronts denial more effectively than private self-examination.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of handling an A-trade that fails?
Answers: When a trade that meets all criteria — all three screens aligned, Iron Triangle sizing, pre-set stops — fails and hits the stop, Elder teaches that this is not a psychological failure but a normal statistical event. The trade was correct in process; the outcome was random. The psychological response should be to record the trade, note that the process was sound, and return to scanning for the next A-trade without emotional contamination from the result.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of a trader who never keeps records?
Answers: A trader who keeps no records is, in Elder's framework, in active denial about their performance — exactly like an alcoholic who refuses to count their drinks. Without records, the mind selectively remembers wins and minimises losses, creating a flattering but false self-image that prevents any realistic assessment of what is working and what must change. Records force honesty; their absence enables comfortable self-deception.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder connect trading psychology to his vision of personal freedom?
Answers: Elder's vision of trading as a path to freedom — living and working anywhere in the world, independent of routine and hierarchy — is explicitly conditional on psychological mastery. The freedom is not available to the undisciplined amateur; it belongs only to the trader who has done the demanding psychological work of self-knowledge, emotional regulation, and rule-following that makes consistent profitability possible. Psychology is both the path to and the price of trading freedom.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: What does Elder say about the psychology of why most traders fail?
Answers: Elder identifies two major causes of trader failure: the inability to control risk and the inability to manage themselves psychologically. Lack of knowledge is a distant third, because even traders with excellent technical knowledge consistently fail if these two foundational skills are absent. The implication is that psychological self-management and risk discipline are more important investments of a beginning trader's time and energy than the study of any additional technical tool.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf

Questions: How does Elder summarise his entire psychology framework in the context of the Three Ms?
Answers: Elder's Three Ms — Mind, Method, and Money — form a hierarchy in which Mind is the enabling condition for the other two. Without psychological stability and discipline, Method cannot be executed consistently and Money management cannot be followed reliably. The Mind is not just one of three equal components; it is the substrate that makes the other two components functional. Mastering the Mind is therefore the primary psychological and practical goal of the trader's development.
Label: Psychology
Source: The New Trading for a Living – Elder (2014) | https://procapital.mohdfaiz.com/books/books-image/mainBook/The%20New%20Trading%20for%20a%20Living.pdf
